tests.intent: The reviewed internal DOOM_TIC_TX.APPLY_BATCH procedure accepts a token and compact command CLOB and returns one cached response BLOB.
Envelope and command objects use version one, exact keys, integer domains, one through four commands, and the sixty-four KiB byte ceiling.
Accepted command sequences are consecutive both inside each batch and against the session last-command sequence under a session row lock.
Every accepted command advances exactly one persisted logical tic at the fixed thirty-five Hertz contract without reading elapsed wall-clock time.
Pause, menu, automap, and cheat controls are applied before later tic stages and their outputs retain deterministic control ordering.
Events within each tic have dense zero-based ordinals in reviewed semantic order and append-only primary keys remain unique.
Every accepted input is stored once with its tic, ordinal, normalized fields, and independent canonical per-command SHA-256.
A successful batch stores its exact range, canonical batch hash, state hash, tic range, and byte-identical response for deterministic retry.
An exact retry of the last accepted range and canonical content returns cached bytes without advancing state or duplicating commands or events.
A repeated accepted range with different canonical content raises conflict and leaves all session state, input, output, and history rows unchanged.
An uncached range at or below the accepted sequence frontier raises old-command error and atomically applies no row changes.
A range starting above the next required sequence raises gap error and atomically applies no state, command, event, history, or cache rows.
Invalid syntax, extra or missing keys, noninteger controls, bad ranges, oversized input, empty arrays, and five-command batches all roll back.
Canonical logical state hashing covers ordered session, player, object, sector, line, mover, switch, RNG, and Appendix F version data but not token identity.
Two different session tokens with identical initial logical rows and command documents produce identical ordered rows, events, and state hashes.
Applying a consecutive command stream one at a time or in reviewed batches reaches identical logical state and ordered command/event rows.
Two real Oracle sessions submitting one identical batch concurrently serialize on the session row and both receive identical bytes while applying once.
Two real Oracle sessions submitting conflicting content for one range produce exactly one commit and one conflict with no mixed partial state.
If response payload construction raises, no transaction-owned logical state, input, event, history, or response row is committed.
Set-based transaction source, anti-cheat restrictions, integrity ancestry, concurrency health, and all semantic mutations fail closed.
